item.title: Strengthening BRSR for climate transition plan disclosures in India - IEEFA
item.description:
Embedding transition planning elements within the existing Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) framework through a phased approach, supported by clear guidance, offers a practical path to improving the credibility and usability of corporate transition plan disclosures in India.

In the first phase, a transition plan snapshot disclosure can be introduced as a short, integrated summary populated from data already required under BRSR and voluntarily published by companies outside the framework.

The second phase can add a focused set of new metrics to BRSR over time, prioritising those that are material, feasible to disclose, and aligned with the International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) Sustainability Disclosure Standards and the Transition Plan Taskforce (TPT) framework.

The phased structure closely follows the rollout pattern of BRSR Core, allowing companies to build internal capacity, data systems, and assurance practices while regulators refine guidance.

This briefing note builds on two previous reports by the Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis (IEEFA). The first report, titled Bridging the net-zero gap, is an empirical assessment of transition plan disclosures by 33 Indian companies across six high-emitting sectors. The second, titled Corporate climate transition planning and disclosures in India, is a comparative mapping of Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) and the International Sustainability Standards Board’s (ISSB) International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) S2 Climate-related Disclosures standard against IEEFA’s transition plan assessment framework. Both reports concluded that the BRSR provides a strong base for sustainability disclosures but does not, in its current form, support the forward-looking, financially integrated transition plan information that capital providers such as lenders and investors increasingly require.

This note translates those findings into a concrete, two-phase pathway that the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) and Indian companies can use to embed transition planning within BRSR. The objective is not to create a parallel disclosure regime, but to make BRSR fit for the purpose by reorganising existing disclosures, supplementing them with a small set of priority metrics, and sequencing the introduction of new requirements in a manner sensitive to corporate sector readiness.

item.title: Cheaper power or climate risk? Pasifika divided over NZ First's billion-dollar oil plan
item.description:
Russel Norman (left) and Esther Tofilau-Tevaga.

Photo/Greenpeace/Facebook/PMN Composite

A Sāmoan New Zealand First candidate says new offshore energy exploration could help ease cost pressures for families but climate campaigners warn Pacific communities could pay the price.

Pacific families struggling with rising living costs are at the centre of a debate over New Zealand First’s plan to spend $1 billion exploring offshore energy resources.

The party says its proposed National Subsurface Development Survey would help Aotearoa better understand its energy resources, strengthen energy security and create a pathway towards lower power prices.

At the party’s election campaign launch on the weekend, NZ First Leader Winston Peters announced that the plan would create a national dataset covering energy, geothermal, and CO₂ storage sites, with the goal of identifying potential deep sea resources over the next two years.

The party says any future extraction would include royalties of more than 50 per cent, with money going towards a Norwegian-model sovereign wealth fund.

Speaking with William Terite on Pacific Mornings, Esther Tofilau-Tevaga, the Chief Executive of a Manurewa charitable foundation and New Zealand First candidate for Manurewa, defended the policy.

She said Pacific families need practical solutions as they deal with cost pressures.

“Peters is someone that has [plenty of] experience. So when he speaks of something, it's something that's going to contribute and give value to New Zealand. He wouldn't just pluck out a policy and put it together,” Tofilau-Tevaga says.

Watch Esther Tofilau-Tevaga’s full interview below.

She says she supports the proposal because of the potential economic benefits.

“If Winston Peters says it's something good, then we believe it's good. If it's going to create more [economy] for the good of our nation, then I tell you it's good.

“I back what Winston Peters has put forward and I support that 100 per cent because he can foresee the goodness of what we need, especially what's happening around the world with the cost of petrol and everything.”

Tofilau-Tevaga says Peters has also supported issues important to Pacific people, including backing the Sāmoan citizenship bill.

New Zealand First leader Winston Peters addresses the party's campaign launch on Sunday in Auckland. Photo/RNZ/Marika Khabazi

But environmental campaigners say the policy could create more problems than solutions.

Russel Norman, Executive Director of Greenpeace Aotearoa, tells Terite that spending public money on offshore oil and gas exploration is a “lose-lose gamble” for New Zealand and the wider Pacific.

“Let's say they go offshore and taxpayers hand over a billion dollars to oil companies. They find new oil, which then gets burnt, then that makes climate change more catastrophic than it already is.

“Let’s say we give them a billion dollars of taxpayer money and they don't find oil, then we've spent a billion dollars for nothing. So either way, it's New Zealanders who are going to lose.

“Imagine if we'd spent a billion dollars supporting renewable energy and storage. It'd be great for energy sovereignty and be great for climate change.”

Listen to Russel Norman’s full interview below.

Norman says expanding fossil fuel use would have serious consequences for Pacific nations already dealing with the impacts of climate change.

Tofilau-Tevaga says she accepts climate change is real but believes the focus should also be on economic opportunities and supporting workers.

“I believe those that have come on, especially someone experienced like Alfred Ngaro that's been with National, it's about our Pacific looking for self-determination,” she says.

“With the other parties, if there's nothing happening there, [it’s] because they draw by party line. With New Zealand First, it is so easy.

“We are one family… and not just a person to add a bit of colour or flavour to the party. This is a party that's real different. It's a party that has experience. It’s a party that talks common sense and it’s a party I now call my family.”

The policy has also drawn criticism from political opponents. National’s energy spokesperson, Simeon Brown, told RNZ that the proposal is “peanuts” compared with previous government decisions on offshore exploration.

Meanwhile, NZ First Deputy Leader Shane Jones has confirmed he is contesting the Northland seat as Peters rules out working with Labour in 2026. He insists developing deep sea reserves is central to economic sovereignty.

item.title: Environmental authorisation for Davis research station infrastructure upgrades
item.description:
Antarctic conservation and protection

The proposed Davis Critical Infrastructure Works (DCIW) will replace end-of-life water, power and mechanical services at Davis. This will help to ensure that the station can safely operate at full capacity and continue to support our world-leading science.

Managing the environmental risk is a vital part of any activity in Antarctica. It is governed by Australia’s Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act 1999 (Cth) and our international obligations under the Antarctic Treaty system.

As part of this process, the Australian Antarctic Division worked with science and ecology experts to assess potential environmental impacts across the life of the project and identify ways to reduce or remove that impact. This culminated in the Initial Environmental Evaluation (IEE): Antarctic Infrastructure Renewal Program – Davis Critical Infrastructure Works, which was submitted in December 2025.

The IEE was reviewed by the Antarctic and Environmental Regulation Section and was open for public comment in early 2026.

The final step was review by the Delegate of the Minister for the Environment and Water, acting under sub-section 12N(2) of the Antarctic Treaty (Environment Protection) Act 1980 (ATEP Act).

The IEE was authorised by the Delegate on 26 June 2026 and is available online via the Secretariat of the Antarctic Treaty website, EIA Database: Initial Environmental Evaluation Antarctic Infrastructure Renewal Program – Davis Critical Infrastructure Works.

Heading south

Works are continuing in Australia with procurement, logistics planning and pre-fabrication in Australia.

Subject to finalisation of the AAD season plan, cargo and personnel will depart Hobart for Davis in October 2026, with works on the icy continent due to begin in November 2026.

As set out in the IEE, strict protocols will be in place to protect the delicate environment and wildlife. This includes biosecurity measures, wildlife protections and care for terrestrial communities and remediation activities. The works are expected to be completed in 2032 with all waste material brought back to Australia for recycling or approved disposal, to close the loop on our environmental considerations.

The AIRP is designed and delivered by the Antarctic Infrastructure Renewal (AIR) Alliance – a four-way partnership between the Australian Antarctic Division, Bouygues Construction Australia, Mott MacDonald Australia and Stantec Australia.
